text: fat is 0.4g. the fat quality is doing a lot of the work here. for a fruit, being sweet is normal, but controlling it is what matters.
carbs is 75g. the fibre support is doing a lot of the work. for fruit, the real question is whether the sweetness stays under control.
protein is 2.5g. protein quality is a real strength here. for a fruit, you want sweetness to be kept in check.
vitamin c at 0% DV, vitamin a at 3% DV stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.
potassium at 15% DV, magnesium at 11% DV, iron at 6% DV stand out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

the upsides first. still brings some useful minerals because not nutritionally empty despite the weak fruit fit. can be useful in high-energy contexts because there are niche cases where the dense sugar load is useful. still a recognisable whole fruit because that matters for fairness even when the category fit is weak.

on the weaker side. sugar density is a major category problem because fruit is supposed to moderate sugar with fibre and payoff, and dates push the sugar side extremely hard. glycemic behaviour is rough for fruit because much weaker fit than fruits with gentler carb behaviour. dried-fruit concentration makes the sugar hit even harsher because this is the cleanest reason dates should remain below ordinary whole fruits like grapes.
C tier. Best only when the convenience or taste matters more than the nutrition return.
